title: Chapter Eight: The White Qilin
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

The problem came with the second match.

The unconscious opponent was dragged away. Yeon-shin stood in the ring and felt what was coming from the opposite side.

*There are many monsters in the world.*

It felt like encountering a martial artist who would one day become an absolute master like the Tyrant Sword Lord.

A man in white walking toward him with the steady gait of someone who had never been rushed. Slender jaw with lines so clean they evoked the blade of a treasured sword. Pitch-black eyes with clear focus — the eyes of a swordsman. Skin white as his clothing, a high nose revealing nobility. A peerlessly handsome young man. Perhaps twenty years old.

*White Qilin. Namgung Hwa-shin.*

He did not need to ask. There was no one else it could be.

The Desolate Sect masters stirred in their seats.

"Which faction will take him? The great lords will fight over him."

"He seems stronger than us. As expected of the Namgung clan."

"Will we see the Boundless Sword of the Azure Sky today?"

They spoke as if his acceptance were decided. Some in the opposite garden looked at Yeon-shin with pity.

*Five years older. A mountain I could climb. Someday.*

Namgung Hwa-shin's energy was beautiful — the flow of inner strength incredibly well-refined. Years of circulating energy through every meridian. Spirit medicines consumed. Truly powerful inner strength breathing within him.

*My body is the only thing better. Everything else is lacking.*

The White Qilin stopped five paces away. His cupped fist salute carried the dignity of an unapproachable noble house.

"I am Namgung Hwa-shin of the Namgung clan. I trained in our family's Boundless Sword of the Azure Sky and Infinite Steps. I look forward to our match."

"Jeong Yeon-shin. Jeong family, Henan. Inner energy cultivation and swift sword."

Bows exchanged.

Namgung Hwa-shin drew his sword. The blade sang — a clear cry, the radiance stretching into the crisp morning air. A silvery arc that cut diagonally through the sky. Dazzling.

The drawing of the sword as a measure of the swordsman. And Namgung Hwa-shin's draw was beautiful.

Yeon-shin did not draw.

*The key to the sword drawing technique is unpredictability. A strike from an unexpected breath. If it lands well, it can catch even a dragon like this off guard.*

He stood still. Circulated his inner energy. Waited.

"Begin," the examiner said.

Clang.

One half of Yeon-shin's sword flew away, severed cleanly, spinning through the air, catching the light before burying itself in the dirt.

He lowered his arm. The broken blade hung at his side.

Commotion erupted from both gardens.

"What happened?"

"Did the boy lose?"

"White Qilin — what a terrifying swift sword!"

The examinees reacted with bewilderment or confident assertions. But the Desolate Sect masters were quiet. All of them. Silent as mice, their earlier looseness replaced by a solemn aura that pressed outward from the ring like heat from a forge.

All staring at Yeon-shin.

Only at Yeon-shin.

Namgung Hwa-shin lowered his sword.

"I lost."

A declaration of defeat. Yeon-shin understood what had happened. He had unleashed the swift sword at full speed the instant the match began — every fibre of speed concentrated into a single strike. Namgung Hwa-shin, wanting to fight at full strength, had imbued his blade with inner energy. Their swords collided. His blade broke — but his strike had arrived first.

Namgung Hwa-shin admitted defeat because the boy's sword was faster. The broken blade was a matter of quality, not skill. Had the swords been equal, the White Qilin would have been cut.

The masters spoke.

"Jeong Yeon-shin, was it? Truly a terrifying swift sword."

"Namgung Hwa-shin had just passed twenty."

"The boy looks five years younger."

"A hidden dragon in Henan. His age is more frightening than his sword."

"He doesn't seem to have learned how to imbue his sword with inner energy. Shall I teach him?"

"Annihilation Thread — don't act rashly. What place wouldn't covet such talent?"

"Don't order me around. He'd be better with us than rotting in your Demon Light Wing."

"Enough," the examiner said.

Silence fell. Yeon-shin found it unexpected. The examiner had the strongest aura among them — but he was not the one who had tried to recruit the boy. The higher-ups handled responsible matters. He was beginning to understand Desolate Sect's character.

He turned to Namgung Hwa-shin. Cupped his fists.

"I hope we can have a proper match next time."

Namgung Hwa-shin smiled. Only interest and joy on his face. Not a trace of dark emotion. His expression was peculiar — a man who had just lost, and felt nothing but curiosity about the one who had beaten him.

"That is what I wanted to say. Let's look forward to it."

*Quite different from my brothers.*

Yeon-shin gathered his broken sword and walked north.

---

In the garden of the victors, he sat among the successful examinees and paid them no mind. He held the broken blade and replayed the strike. Weight distribution of both feet. Layering of inner energy at the critical moment. Explosion of contracted muscles. For now, he could see no way to improve further.

The sword was the problem.

*Demon Light Wing was right. I don't know how to imbue a sword with inner energy.*

Of course he did not. The Jeong family manor had been third-rate — a house pretending at influence in a backwater county. No one in the family had known such a skill. If they had, they could never have kept it to themselves. The Jeong family disliked silence as much as they disliked change.

*But now that I know it exists, that's enough.*

Inner energy flowed from his hand into the broken blade. He felt the iron — how it could be permeated, saturated, enveloped. The energy did not stop at the surface. It seeped into the grain of the metal, binding to each fibre as if the sword were an extension of his meridians.

Not just imbuing. Becoming one.

His consciousness sank into the weapon. The broken edge was his broken edge. The tang was his spine. The blade called him deeper.

Wooong.

The sword vibrated. A sound like crying — low and resonant, rising from the metal itself. Not from any strike or motion. From the unity.

Sword cry.

"Sword cry!" someone shouted. The examinees leapt to their feet. Yeon-shin's eyes were closed. He did not hear them. He felt the vibration singing through his palm, up his arm, into his bones, and he let it come.

*Unity of body and sword. It really does feel like becoming one.*

The swift sword with this new precision — the blade responding not to muscle alone but to intention. Energy and steel moving as one entity. The speed would not change, but the control would be absolute.

He was gaining something with every step since the test. The fruit of the World Tree was still far off. But this was more than enough for today.

*Desolate Sect. A good place.*

When he opened his eyes, Namgung Hwa-shin stood beside him. One hand resting on his sword hilt. Ready. He had been standing guard — keeping others from disturbing the enlightenment. A gentle smile on his face, and beneath it something that looked very much like respect.

"Congratulations on your achievement."

"I didn't realise you were standing guard. Thank you. And congratulations to you as well on passing, Young Master Namgung."

For a moment the White Qilin's face went blank — surprise, genuine and unguarded. Then he laughed. Warm, open, delighted.

*One could wonder if a person could be this upright.*

There were others who looked on enviously, wanting to befriend the White Qilin. But Yeon-shin's gaze had already returned to his sword.

---

Half a shichen later, Won-chang appeared.

He had passed. He had taken the test several times before, and finally, the mountain had yielded. The sight of him tapping the dragon on his headband — tapping it over and over, giddy and disbelieving — was almost endearing.

"I've decided on a title," he announced. "How about 'Divine Hero of Desolate Sect'?"

"Are there cases where one chooses their own title?"

"Why not? The martial world is full of brazen men who introduce themselves by their own titles. If I say I am the Divine Hero, then that's my title."

"Then every swordsman would already be a Sword Saint."

Won-chang pretended not to hear.

Namgung Hwa-shin approached. "It seems Young Master Jeong is already being called something by others."

"Me? But I've only taken the test."

"Lightning Flash. If the Desolate Sect masters are calling you that directly, it is already a title in the murim."

Lightning Flash.

*I could not have imagined this while sitting in horse stance at the Jeong family manor.*

"Somewhat fast, you say?" Namgung Hwa-shin's eyes were bright with amusement. "Then what of myself, who lost track of your breath? There are many cases where a martial artist's potential is captured in their title. Congratulations."

"Thank you."

Yeon-shin replied with a slightly disgruntled expression that did not quite hide his satisfaction.

The self-proclaimed Divine Hero of Desolate Sect looked on, envious.

---
